news_summary: Recent discoveries suggest that an ancient monument could be an early prototype of Stonehenge, shedding new light on prehistoric architecture and cultural development.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes laissent penser qu'un monument antique pourrait être un prototype précoce de Stonehenge, apportant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur l'architecture préhistorique et le développement culturel.
news_summary_es: Los últimos descubrimientos sugieren que un monumento antiguo podría ser un prototipo temprano de Stonehenge, lo que arroja nueva luz sobre la arquitectura prehistórica y el desarrollo cultural.

articles.title: Ancient monument may have been an early Stonehenge prototype
articles.summary: Archaeologists have discovered traces of a wooden structure built 5000 years ago, 5 kilometres from Stonehenge, which appears to have been an even older monument for marking the summer solstice
articles.content:
Archaeologists have discovered traces of a wooden structure built 5000 years ago, 5 kilometres from Stonehenge, which appears to have been an even older monument for marking the summer solstice

A reconstruction of the summer solstice celebrations as they might have appeared at Bulford thousands of years ago Marijane Porter, Wessex Archaeology

Stone Age peoples in Britain built a wooden monument to mark the summer solstice, 500 years before they began building the stone circle at Stonehenge.

Stonehenge is also aligned to the summer solstice, and the wooden monument may have been an early prototype of this. It is one of the earliest examples of a monument aligned to an astronomical phenomenon in the British Isles.

“What we have now, for the first time, is actual proof that these people were capable of capturing the movement of the sun,” said Phil Harding at Wessex Archaeology, who led the excavations, at a press conference announcing the discovery.

Stonehenge is a monument built during the Neolithic, the very end of the Stone Age. Situated on Salisbury Plain in the UK, it consists of an outer ring of vertical sarsen stones topped by horizontal lintels, a smaller inner ring of vertical bluestones and several other stones. These are surrounded by an earth bank and a ditch. This is the oldest part, built around 3100 BC, with the stones being placed over the centuries up to 1600 BC.

Some of the standing stones seem to have been carefully aligned to point to the spot on the horizon where the sun rises on the summer solstice, and to the opposing spot where the sun sets on the winter solstice. The relevant stones were erected about 2500 BC.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

Harding and his colleagues have found the remains of a nearby monument that is 500 years older. About 5 kilometres north-east of Stonehenge, there is a village called Bulford, where the UK’s Ministry of Defence wanted to house about 5000 army personnel. Before construction began, Wessex Archaeology excavated Bulford from 2015 to 2017.

The team found a cluster of pits containing grooved ware pottery, which was made by late Neolithic peoples. Radiocarbon dating suggested the pottery was from about 2950 BC. The researchers obtained 40 dates, all tightly clustered in time. “This site was being occupied for a relatively short period of time,” said Harding. “It could be something like a decade.”

“It’s a really important Middle Neolithic settlement,” says Susan Greaney at the University of Exeter, UK, who wasn’t involved in the study.

A piece of pottery found at Bulford, UK Wessex Archaeology

Two of the Bulford pits were a different shape to the others. Instead of having vertical sides, they tapered towards the bottom, going from about 1.2 metres across to just 0.5 metres. They also didn’t contain pottery, but were filled with chalk rubble. Harding and his colleagues concluded that they were postholes: they had once housed timbers a few metres tall, which were held upright by the rubble. In line with this, one of the postholes contained charcoal from an ash tree.

The two postholes are about 120 metres apart. Harding realised that a line drawn through them would point roughly north-east, at 48.1 degrees: about the direction of the midsummer sunrise. “I got really, really excited about that,” said Harding.

Wessex Archaeology recruited Fabio Silva, a skyscape archaeologist at consultancy Stone x Sky, to study the alignment of the postholes more carefully. Using a 3D reconstruction of the landscape with modern buildings removed, plus data on the sun’s shifting path across the sky, Silva determined that the postholes were neatly aligned with the past summer solstice sunrise.

Strictly, the alignment was about 1 degree out, but Silva said this makes sense once you realise that the wooden posts could have been up to 50 centimetres across. “You have to take that width into account,” he said at the press conference, in which case the alignment is “bang on”. “The odds of this being by chance are less than 0.5 per cent,” he said.

“Probably a rough orientation is good enough for the ritual that you are supposed to carry out in these sites,” says A. César González-García at the Spanish National Research Council in Santiago de Compostela, who wasn’t involved in the study. “It looks like there is a broad understanding and interest in the sky.”

Older sites in the area also show evidence of people tracking the sun, albeit with less precision. “From the earliest times that we have Neolithic people present in that landscape, they are incorporating the sun into their ceremonial architecture,” said Matt Leivers, also at Wessex Archaeology.

“We’ve got loads of timber monuments that have those kind of alignments,” says Greaney. The Bulford monument “is adding another one to that, potentially, but much earlier”.

For instance, at nearby Larkhill, there is a Neolithic enclosure from around 3700 BC, well before Bulford and Stonehenge. Its entrance faces roughly north-east. If you stand in that entrance and look at Sidbury Hill (the highest point on the horizon) on midsummer morning, “you see the sun rise dead ahead of you over Sidbury Hill”, said Leivers.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery of a 5000-year-old wooden monument aligned with the summer solstice, predating Stonehenge and providing new insights into early human understanding of astronomy. This finding has broad cultural and historical significance, enriching knowledge about ancient human societies and their relationship with natural phenomena, which is inspiring and uplifting for society.
articles.personality_title: Discovery of 5,000-year-old wooden monument predating Stonehenge reveals early sun-tracking skills
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Stonehenge is a famous ancient stone circle in the UK, built about 4,000 years ago. It is known for being aligned with the summer solstice, the longest day of the year, when the sun rises in a special spot on the horizon.

**What happened** – Archaeologists found remains of a wooden monument near Stonehenge, at a place called Bulford, that is about 5,000 years old. This means it was built 500 years before Stonehenge. The monument had tall wooden posts placed in the ground, which were arranged to line up with the sunrise on the summer solstice. The discovery was made during an excavation from 2015 to 2017 before army housing was built.

**Impact** – This wooden monument shows that people living in Britain during the Stone Age could track the sun’s movements even before Stonehenge was built. It proves they understood how to mark important times of the year using the sun’s position. This is one of the earliest examples of such an astronomical alignment in the British Isles, giving new insight into how ancient people connected with nature.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying this site and other nearby ancient places to learn more about how early communities observed the sky. This could help explain how monuments like Stonehenge were inspired by earlier wooden structures and improve our understanding of ancient cultures.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A 5,000-year-old wooden monument near Stonehenge reveals that ancient people were marking the summer solstice centuries before the famous stone circle was built.
articles.personality_title_fr: Découverte d’un monument en bois vieux de 5 000 ans, antérieur à Stonehenge, révèle une maîtrise ancienne du suivi du soleil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Stonehenge est un célèbre cercle de pierres en Angleterre, construit il y a environ 4 000 ans. Il est connu pour être aligné avec le solstice d’été, le jour le plus long de l’année, lorsque le soleil se lève à un endroit précis à l’horizon.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des archéologues ont découvert les restes d’un monument en bois près de Stonehenge, à Bulford, vieux d’environ 5 000 ans. Cela signifie qu’il a été construit 500 ans avant Stonehenge. Le monument comportait de grands poteaux en bois placés dans le sol, alignés avec le lever du soleil lors du solstice d’été. Cette découverte a eu lieu lors de fouilles menées entre 2015 et 2017 avant la construction de logements militaires.

**Impact** – Ce monument en bois montre que les habitants de l’Angleterre à l’époque de la pierre pouvaient suivre les mouvements du soleil bien avant Stonehenge. Cela prouve qu’ils comprenaient comment marquer des moments importants de l’année grâce à la position du soleil. C’est l’un des premiers exemples d’un tel alignement astronomique dans les îles britanniques, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur la relation des anciens avec la nature.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier ce site et d’autres lieux anciens proches pour mieux comprendre comment les premières communautés observaient le ciel. Cela pourrait expliquer comment des monuments comme Stonehenge ont été inspirés par des structures en bois plus anciennes et améliorer notre compréhension des cultures anciennes.

**En une phrase** – Un monument en bois vieux de 5 000 ans près de Stonehenge révèle que les anciens marquaient le solstice d’été des siècles avant la construction du célèbre cercle de pierres.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubrimiento de monumento de madera de 5.000 años anterior a Stonehenge revela habilidades tempranas para seguir el sol
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Stonehenge es un famoso círculo de piedras en el Reino Unido, construido hace unos 4.000 años. Es conocido por estar alineado con el solsticio de verano, el día más largo del año, cuando el sol sale en un lugar especial en el horizonte.

**Qué pasó** – Arqueólogos encontraron restos de un monumento de madera cerca de Stonehenge, en un lugar llamado Bulford, que tiene unos 5.000 años. Esto significa que fue construido 500 años antes que Stonehenge. El monumento tenía postes de madera altos colocados en el suelo, alineados con la salida del sol en el solsticio de verano. El hallazgo se realizó durante excavaciones entre 2015 y 2017 antes de construir viviendas militares.

**Impacto** – Este monumento de madera muestra que las personas que vivían en Gran Bretaña durante la Edad de Piedra podían seguir el movimiento del sol incluso antes de construir Stonehenge. Prueba que entendían cómo marcar momentos importantes del año usando la posición del sol. Es uno de los primeros ejemplos de esta clase de alineación astronómica en las islas británicas, dando nueva información sobre cómo los antiguos se relacionaban con la naturaleza.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando este y otros sitios antiguos cercanos para aprender más sobre cómo las primeras comunidades observaban el cielo. Esto podría ayudar a explicar cómo monumentos como Stonehenge fueron inspirados por estructuras de madera anteriores y mejorar nuestro conocimiento de las culturas antiguas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un monumento de madera de 5.000 años cerca de Stonehenge revela que los antiguos marcaban el solsticio de verano siglos antes de que se construyera el famoso círculo de piedras.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a prehistoric wooden monument with tall timber posts casting long shadows aligned perfectly to the glowing horizon where the summer solstice sun rises over a gently rolling Salisbury Plain landscape near Stonehenge, with scattered Neolithic pottery shards embedded in the earth around the posts, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and ochres.
